nativeRange.habitat: Typically found near steep drop-offs and caves at depths of 200 to 400 metres, though on moonless nights individuals migrate into shallower coral-reef waters. The species is reef-associated throughout its range in the Indo-Pacific and Central Pacific.

diet.notes: Naturally feeds on zooplankton, small crustaceans, and other small invertebrates, using bioluminescent light organs to detect prey in darkness. In captivity, has been reported to accept mysis shrimp, krill, and other meaty foods, but typically only after lights-out; several small feedings per day are generally advised.

imageCandidates.notes: Gubbi0us; 504x335. Whole live individual sharply in frame showing the dark silvery body and, crucially, the white bioluminescent light organ glowing beneath the eye (the defining diagnostic feature). Low resolution is the only caveat.
Gubbi0us; 367x240. School of many individuals over reef with light organs visible, representing the species' nocturnal shoaling behaviour. Low resolution.

Robin van der Vliet; 4032x3024. Darkened aquarium shot capturing the signature bioluminescent blue-green flashes of a group; shows the defining glow but not the fish bodies (atmospheric supporting image).

summary: The Two-Fin Flashlight Fish produces light from symbiotic bacteria housed in rotating organs beneath each eye, which it uses to signal, hunt, and confuse predators. Generally regarded as a specialist species, it does best in small groups in dimly lit, expert-level aquariums.
careNotes: Bioluminescent organs beneath each eye host symbiotic Vibrio bacteria that emit a steady glow; the fish can rotate each organ to flash it on and off, using this ability to communicate with others of its kind, detect prey in darkness, and confuse predators. The Two-Fin Flashlight Fish is generally considered strictly nocturnal — it hides inside caves or overhangs during the day and emerges only after lights-out — so the aquarium should offer ample shaded rockwork, deep cave structures, and subdued lighting, or be run on a moonlight-only photoperiod. It is best kept in a small group of three or more alongside other nocturnal species; individuals kept alone tend to do poorly. The species is reported to be extremely sensitive to copper-based medications and should never be exposed to them.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided care level, diet, water parameters, minimum tank size, temperament, reef-compatibility, copper sensitivity. The collection landing URL supplied by the discovery agent did not list this species; the canonical product page was located by search and used in its place.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperament, native range, diet, care level, reef-compatibility. Reported adult size 14 in (~36 cm), agreeing with FishBase/Wikipedia wild maximum but disagreeing with LiveAquaria's 9 in.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy (family Anomalopidae, order Trachichthyiformes), max length 35 cm TL, preferred temperature 17.9–27.8 °C (mean 24.1 °C), distribution, depth, diet (zooplankton).

additional.name: Wikipedia (Splitfin flashlightfish)
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 35 cm, nocturnal schooling behavior, distribution from Philippines/Indonesia to Tuamotus, light-organ rotation mechanism.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Provided specialist tank-size guidance (2000 L minimum), temperature range 22–27 °C, and confirmation of strictly nocturnal habit; identifies the species as suitable only for advanced aquarists.
